summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Right big toe pain and redness.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports a two-week history of intermittent right big toe pain, initially mild but now significantly painful. Last week, the toe became red and warm to the touch, particularly when walking. He describes the pain as throbbing, occasionally sharp, and exacerbated by movement or prolonged weight-bearing. He reports difficulty walking the halls at a recent trade show convention and mentions needing to stand still. He denies any recent injury or trauma. He has been taking ibuprofen twice daily with limited relief. He has never had problems with his stomach. He uses regular shoes and a regular pedal and has never had any problems with his stomach. He enjoys fishing and catching blue croppies and he wants to continue working and doing his regular activities.

Findings
Examination reveals a large spur and significant cartilage loss in the right big toe joint. There is evidence of arthritis, characterized by pain with palpation and movement, particularly plantar flexion and pushing the toe into the adjacent toe. Bone spurs are palpable. The patient reports pain with pressure on the outside of the toe and also on the inside of the toe. The joint moves approximately 20 degrees with pain.

Assessment
The patient's symptoms and examination findings are consistent with hallux rigidus with significant arthritis and cartilage loss in the right big toe joint.

Plan
Prescribe an orthotic insert for the patient's shoe. Start meloxicam, 1mg daily. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess treatment response. If symptoms do not improve, consider a shot or surgical intervention to clean out the joint. Encourage the patient to continue working and doing his regular activities and to test the orthotics and medication. Schedule a follow-up in two weeks to assess treatment response.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right great toe pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Joseph Walker is a pleasant 58-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right great toe pain. The onset of his pain began 2 weeks ago, however it worsened last week. He noticed the pain worsening when he was at a trade show convention and he could not ambulate as he was forced to stand there as the pain was there the whole time. He denies any specific injury. The patient describes his pain usually as throbbing and burning, but notes it occasionally changes to sharp, stabbing pain especially with movement or prolonged ambulation. His symptoms also include redness to the right great toe. The patient states that he has been ambulating on his heel to keep his toe from bending. He reports that his pain is present even with the slightest of pressure, which he notes is worse at night when his sheet is touching his right toe. He adds that he has been taking 2 ibuprofen per day, which does not provide him with relief.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports that he likes to bicycle ride.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right great toe pain.
Skin: Reports redness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the right great toe reveals 10 degrees of plantar flexion with pain. Pain to palpation of the right great toe, between the big toe and 2nd toe. Palpated a bone spur on the right great toe.

RESULTS

X-ray of the right great toe taken today in office reveals a large bone spur on the anterior aspect of the right great toe joint. There is a loss of cartilage with some arthritis present.

ASSESSMENT

Right foot hallux rigidus.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays revealed hallux rigidus. We discussed treatment options for this and I have recommended that we begin with conservative treatment in the form of custom orthotics. I have also prescribed the patient meloxicam once a day to treat the pain. The patient was instructed to discontinue use and contact the office if gastrointestinal issues develop. I advised the patient that I want him to continue his regular activities.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 2 weeks to check on his progress. If his pain does not improve with the orthotics, I will recommend a cortisone injection or surgical intervention.

section_miscategorizations.text: Examination reveals a large spur and significant cartilage loss in the right big toe joint. There is evidence of arthritis, characterized by pain with palpation and movement, particularly plantar flexion and pushing the toe into the adjacent toe. Bone spurs are palpable. The patient reports pain with pressure on the outside of the toe and also on the inside of the toe. The joint moves approximately 20 degrees with pain.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The description of a large spur, significant cartilage loss, and evidence of arthritis synthesizes imaging/exam into a diagnostic impression (hallux rigidus/arthritis) rather than listing discrete objective findings only; however, it is closely tied to the physical exam and not seriously misleading here.
